content.text: The nominative case is used for the subject of a sentence — the doer or performer of the action. In English, we say: 'I threw the ball.' We would never say: 'Me threw the ball,' because 'me' is not used as the subject.
In Greek, nouns are grouped into three main families, called declensions: first, second, and third. Each declension has its own set of nominative case endings.
The first declension mainly contains feminine nouns that share a common pattern of endings in the nominative case.
All of these nouns end in eta (ή). This ending is a common marker for the nominative case in first declension nouns.
